explanation: COUNT counts how many rows or non-NULL values exist. COUNT(*) counts every row including NULLs; COUNT(column) skips NULLs. Example: COUNT(*) on a students table returns total enrolled students, while COUNT(grade) skips students with no grade recorded yet.

SUM adds up all numeric values in a column. NULL values are automatically skipped. Example: SUM(amount) on a sales table gives you total revenue for the period. It only works correctly on numeric columns like INT or FLOAT.

MIN returns the smallest value; MAX returns the largest. They work on numbers, dates and even strings. Example: MAX(salary) finds the highest-paid employee; MIN(order_date) finds the earliest order placed in the system.

AVG divides the sum of a column by the count of non-NULL rows. NULLs are excluded from both numerator and denominator. Example: AVG(score) on an exam table gives the class average, automatically ignoring students who were absent and have NULL scores.

The basic syntax places the aggregate function in the SELECT list. No GROUP BY means the function runs over the entire table and returns one row. Example: SELECT AVG(score) FROM matches returns a single number — the overall average score across all matches.

You can list several aggregates separated by commas in one SELECT. This is efficient and readable. Example: SELECT MIN(price), MAX(price), AVG(price) FROM products gives minimum, maximum and average product price in a single database scan.

WHERE is placed before the aggregation to restrict which rows are included. Only rows matching the condition contribute to the aggregate. Example: SELECT AVG(score) FROM matches WHERE year = 2023 computes average score only for 2023 matches.

COUNT(*) counts every row in the result set regardless of NULL values. COUNT(1) does the same thing — it counts a constant per row. Both return the same result. Example: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM employees returns the total headcount including employees with missing data.

COUNT(column) counts only rows where the specified column is not NULL. This lets you count how many rows have data in a particular field. Example: COUNT(phone_number) in a contacts table tells you how many contacts actually have a phone number stored.

When all input values are NULL, COUNT returns 0 (no rows counted) while SUM, AVG, MIN and MAX return NULL. This is important for defensive coding. Example: AVG(score) returns NULL if every score in the filtered set is NULL — always check for this in reports.

MIN and MAX on string columns use dictionary (alphabetical) ordering. 'Apple' < 'Banana' < 'Zebra'. Example: SELECT MIN(player_name) FROM cricket returns the player whose name comes first alphabetically, not the lowest scorer.

SUM and AVG are designed for numbers. On string columns, behaviour varies: some DBMS raise an error, some cast implicitly, some return NULL. Example: SUM(grade_letter) where grade is 'A'/'B' may error in PostgreSQL but silently return 0 in SQLite.

All aggregate functions (except COUNT(*)) automatically skip NULL values. This means your average or sum reflects only rows with actual data. Example: AVG(salary) with three salaries 50k, NULL, 70k returns 60k — the NULL is excluded from both sum and count.

The AS keyword renames the output column of an expression or aggregate. It makes the result set self-explanatory and easier to reference in application code. Example: SELECT SUM(runs) AS total_runs, AVG(runs) AS avg_runs FROM innings gives clean, readable column names.

Common patterns: SUM(score) shows a player's total runs; MAX(score) finds a player's best performance; MIN(score) finds the lowest. Example: SELECT player, SUM(score) AS total, MAX(score) AS best FROM matches GROUP BY player summarizes each player.

Practice combining aggregates with GROUP BY. Example: SELECT player, year, AVG(score) AS avg_score FROM matches GROUP BY player, year gives average per player per year. SELECT player, SUM(sixes) AS total_sixes FROM matches GROUP BY player counts sixes hit by each player.

CASE checks each WHEN condition in order and returns the first matching THEN value. If no condition matches and ELSE is provided, ELSE value is returned. Example: CASE WHEN age < 18 THEN 'Minor' WHEN age < 60 THEN 'Adult' ELSE 'Senior' END classifies people into age groups.

CASE is versatile — it can appear in SELECT (to create computed columns), ORDER BY (custom sort order), GROUP BY (custom grouping) and HAVING. Example: ORDER BY CASE WHEN status='Active' THEN 1 ELSE 2 END sorts active records first before inactive ones.

If no WHEN condition matches and there is no ELSE clause, CASE returns NULL by default. Always add ELSE to handle unmatched rows explicitly. Example: CASE WHEN score >= 90 THEN 'A' END returns NULL for scores below 90 if ELSE is omitted — add ELSE 'B' to avoid silent NULLs.

This pattern counts only rows matching a condition. CASE returns 1 for matching rows and NULL otherwise; COUNT ignores NULLs so only matches are counted. Example: SELECT COUNT(CASE WHEN gender='F' THEN 1 END) AS female_count FROM employees counts female employees without a WHERE clause.

Combine GROUP BY with CASE aggregates to get breakdowns per group in a single query. Example: SELECT dept, SUM(CASE WHEN score>=50 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS passed FROM exams GROUP BY dept shows how many passed per department in one result set.

CASE is ideal for bucketing continuous values into categories. Example: SELECT CASE WHEN rating >= 4.5 THEN 'Excellent' WHEN rating >= 3 THEN 'Good' ELSE 'Poor' END AS category, COUNT(*) FROM products GROUP BY category counts products in each rating bucket.

UNION merges results and removes duplicate rows (slower, requires sorting). UNION ALL keeps all rows including duplicates (faster). Example: Merging customer lists from two regions with UNION removes duplicates who appear in both; UNION ALL keeps them twice.

INTERSECT returns only rows that appear in both query results. It finds the overlap between two sets. Example: SELECT product_id FROM store_A INTERSECT SELECT product_id FROM store_B returns products sold in both stores.

EXCEPT (called MINUS in Oracle) returns rows from the first query that are not in the second. Example: SELECT user_id FROM registered EXCEPT SELECT user_id FROM purchased finds users who registered but never bought anything.

Both SELECT statements in a set operation must produce the same number of columns and each pair of corresponding columns must have compatible types. Example: Combining SELECT name, age with SELECT name, salary fails if age (INT) and salary (FLOAT) are incompatible — use CAST to align.

SQL matches columns by position, not by name. The first column of SELECT 1 is paired with the first column of SELECT 2. Example: SELECT 'Actor' AS role, name FROM actors UNION SELECT 'Director' AS role, name FROM directors works because both have role and name in the same order.

Real-world use: combine movie and TV show casts. Example: SELECT actor_name FROM movie_cast UNION SELECT actor_name FROM tv_cast gives all unique performers across both media. Add WHERE clauses inside each SELECT to filter before combining.

Each individual SELECT in a set operation cannot have its own ORDER BY. Only one ORDER BY is allowed and it goes after the final SELECT. Example: SELECT name FROM A UNION SELECT name FROM B ORDER BY name — placing ORDER BY before UNION causes a syntax error.

ORDER BY in a set operation should use column names from the first SELECT or column positions (1, 2, 3...). Example: If both SELECTs return (name, score), use ORDER BY 2 DESC to sort by score without ambiguity across both queries.

LIMIT and OFFSET paginate the final combined result, not individual SELECTs. Example: SELECT city FROM us_cities UNION SELECT city FROM uk_cities ORDER BY city LIMIT 10 OFFSET 20 fetches cities 21–30 from the alphabetically sorted combined list.

SQL supports +, -, *, / and % (modulo) for numeric calculations. Division of integers may truncate — use CAST to get decimals. Example: SELECT total_price / quantity AS unit_price FROM orders calculates per-unit cost; SELECT order_id % 2 AS is_even checks if order ID is even.

Mixing data types in expressions follows implicit conversion rules. INT + FLOAT gives FLOAT. Adding a number to a string may error or concatenate depending on DBMS. Example: SELECT 5 + 2.5 returns 7.5 in most DBMS; SELECT '10' + 5 may return 15 or error.

The || operator joins two string values into one. Some DBMS use CONCAT() function instead. Example: SELECT first_name || ' ' || last_name AS full_name FROM employees combines first and last name into a single readable full name column.

In SELECT, expressions create derived columns without storing new data. AS gives the derived column a readable name. Example: SELECT name, salary * 12 AS annual_salary FROM employees computes yearly pay on the fly from monthly salary stored in the table.

WHERE can use arithmetic expressions to filter rows. Example: WHERE price * quantity > 1000 filters orders where the line total exceeds 1000. This avoids creating a stored computed column just for filtering.

UPDATE can use expressions on the right-hand side to compute new values based on existing data. Example: UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance - 500 WHERE account_id = 42 deducts 500 from a specific account without first reading the balance.

HAVING filters grouped results using aggregate expressions. Example: SELECT dept, AVG(salary) AS avg_sal FROM employees GROUP BY dept HAVING AVG(salary) > 60000 shows only departments where the average salary exceeds 60k.

strftime(format, datetime_value) returns the formatted string. %Y = 4-digit year, %m = 2-digit month, %d = 2-digit day, %H = hour. Example: strftime('%Y-%m', created_at) returns '2024-03' — useful for grouping data by month in analytics queries.

strftime results can be used in WHERE to filter by year or month, and in GROUP BY to aggregate by time period. Example: WHERE strftime('%Y', sale_date) = '2023' filters 2023 sales; GROUP BY strftime('%m', sale_date) groups by calendar month.

Monthly and annual reports are common use cases. Example: SELECT strftime('%Y-%m', order_date) AS month, SUM(amount) AS revenue FROM orders GROUP BY month ORDER BY month generates a month-by-month revenue trend report.

CAST takes any expression and converts it to the specified type. Common targets: INT, FLOAT, TEXT, DATE. Example: SELECT CAST(price AS INT) FROM products truncates decimal prices to whole numbers — useful for bucketing or integer arithmetic.

strftime always returns a string. To compare years or months numerically, cast to INT. Example: WHERE CAST(strftime('%Y', sale_date) AS INT) >= 2022 correctly filters rows from 2022 onwards; without CAST, string comparison '9' > '10' could give wrong results.

Cast when comparing a string column to a number, or when arithmetic needs proper numeric types. Example: GROUP BY CAST(score AS INT) groups decimal scores by their integer part, useful for histogram-style breakdowns in score distribution reports.

FLOOR rounds a number down to the nearest integer; CEIL rounds up. Example: FLOOR(4.9) = 4, CEIL(4.1) = 5. Use FLOOR(price/10)*10 to group prices into decade buckets (0–9, 10–19 etc.) for price-range analysis.

ROUND(x, n) rounds x to n decimal places. ROUND(x, 0) rounds to the nearest integer. Example: ROUND(AVG(rating), 1) rounds average product rating to one decimal, like 4.3, making it suitable for display in a product listing page.

Combine ROUND, FLOOR and CEIL with aggregates for clean report output. Example: SELECT player, ROUND(AVG(score), 2) AS avg_score FROM matches GROUP BY player produces a neat leaderboard with averages rounded to 2 decimal places.

UPPER() converts text to all caps; LOWER() converts to all lowercase. Using LOWER(column) in WHERE ensures comparisons are case-insensitive. Example: WHERE LOWER(city) = 'london' matches 'London', 'LONDON' and 'london' all at once in the database.

Wrap columns in LOWER() before LIKE to search case-insensitively. Example: WHERE LOWER(product_name) LIKE '%shirt%' finds 'T-Shirt', 'SHIRT', 'shirt' etc. This is useful when user input might be in any case and data is inconsistently stored.

String functions can reformat data for display. Example: SELECT UPPER(country_code) AS code FROM countries ensures codes like 'us' or 'Us' all appear as 'US' in reports, regardless of how they were originally stored.

All rows with the same value in the GROUP BY column form one group. The aggregate runs once per group, producing one output row per group. Example: GROUP BY country on a sales table creates one group per country so SUM(sales) gives country-level totals.

In SELECT, every non-aggregate column must appear in GROUP BY. Example: SELECT category, COUNT(*) AS item_count FROM products GROUP BY category counts items per category; listing price in SELECT without GROUP BY causes an error in strict DBMS.

GROUP BY enables segment-level analytics instead of one big total. Example: SELECT brand, AVG(price) FROM products GROUP BY brand shows average price per brand — more useful than a single overall average for understanding pricing strategy.

A multi-column GROUP BY treats each distinct combination as a separate group. Example: GROUP BY student_name, subject groups separately for 'Alice - Math', 'Alice - Science', 'Bob - Math' so you can compute each student's per-subject average.

Simply list the columns separated by commas. Both columns must either appear in SELECT or be used in an aggregate. Example: SELECT player, year, SUM(score) FROM matches GROUP BY player, year gives total runs per player per year across all matches.

Classic cricket analytics query: SELECT player, year, SUM(score) AS total_runs FROM matches GROUP BY player, year ORDER BY year, total_runs DESC. This groups by the unique player-year pair and sums scores, giving a season-by-season scorecard for every player.

The WHERE clause is evaluated first, excluding non-matching rows from grouping. The aggregate then only sees the filtered subset. Example: WHERE department = 'Sales' before GROUP BY region ensures only sales team rows are counted per region.

Place WHERE before GROUP BY in the query. Execution order: FROM → WHERE → GROUP BY → SELECT. Example: SELECT category, SUM(price) FROM products WHERE in_stock = 1 GROUP BY category sums only in-stock items per category.

SELECT player, COUNT(*) AS half_centuries FROM innings WHERE score >= 50 GROUP BY player — WHERE filters to only innings of 50+ runs, then GROUP BY player counts how many such innings each player has, producing a half-century tally per player.

SELECT player_name, SUM(score) AS total_score FROM matches GROUP BY player_name returns one row per player with their cumulative score. This is useful for building leaderboards or ranking players by total contribution.

SELECT player, MAX(score) AS best_score, AVG(score) AS avg_score FROM matches GROUP BY player gives each player's best single performance and their overall average — helpful for talent assessment reports.

SELECT player, SUM(sixes) AS total_sixes FROM innings GROUP BY player counts six-hits per player. Alternatively, COUNT(*) counts innings played. These patterns generalize to counting purchases per customer or tickets per event.

HAVING is evaluated after GROUP BY computes aggregates. It removes groups whose aggregate values do not satisfy the condition. Example: HAVING SUM(sales) > 100000 keeps only regions where total sales exceed 100k — impossible to do with WHERE on individual rows.

HAVING follows GROUP BY in the query. Example: SELECT dept, COUNT(*) AS headcount FROM employees GROUP BY dept HAVING COUNT(*) >= 10 lists only departments with 10 or more employees, filtering out smaller teams.

Any aggregate function valid in SELECT can be used in HAVING. Example: HAVING AVG(rating) > 4 filters product categories where the average customer rating exceeds 4 stars — a common e-commerce quality filter.

WHERE runs before aggregation and excludes entire rows. Use it to filter on non-aggregated column values. Example: WHERE country='India' before GROUP BY city limits the analysis to Indian cities only before counting or summing.

HAVING runs after GROUP BY and filters based on group-level results. Use it when the filter depends on an aggregate value. Example: HAVING MAX(price) > 500 keeps only product categories that have at least one item priced above 500.

Logical execution order: FROM → WHERE (filter rows) → GROUP BY (form groups) → aggregate → HAVING (filter groups) → SELECT. Example: SELECT player, AVG(score) FROM matches WHERE format='T20' GROUP BY player HAVING AVG(score) > 30 finds T20 players averaging above 30.

SELECT player, COUNT(*) AS half_centuries FROM innings WHERE score >= 50 GROUP BY player HAVING COUNT(*) > 1 returns players who have scored 50+ runs in more than one inning. HAVING is needed because COUNT(*) > 1 is a group-level condition.

Common patterns: HAVING COUNT(*) >= 3 (active users), HAVING SUM(amount) > 10000 (big-spending customers), HAVING AVG(score) < 30 (struggling players). Example: SELECT vendor, SUM(order_value) AS total FROM orders GROUP BY vendor HAVING SUM(order_value) > 50000 shows high-value vendors.

Data is raw facts or values — numbers, text, dates — that on their own may not have meaning. When organized, data becomes information. Example: '92', 'Alice', '2024-01-15' are individual data points; combined as a student exam record they become meaningful information.

A database is an organized, structured collection of related data stored so it can be easily accessed, managed and updated. Example: An e-commerce database stores tables for customers, orders and products, all linked so that an order row references the correct customer and product.

A DBMS is the software layer between users/applications and the physical data files. It handles storage, querying, access control and backups. Example: MySQL is a DBMS; when your app runs SELECT * FROM users, MySQL finds and returns the data from disk.

DBMS controls who can access which data using roles and permissions. Sensitive data can be encrypted and access logs maintained. Example: A bank DBMS allows tellers to view account balances but not modify them, while only managers can approve large transfers.

DBMS provides SQL as a standard language so users can query complex data without knowing how it is physically stored. Example: A marketing analyst writes SELECT SUM(sales) FROM orders without knowing which disk file stores the orders table.

DBMS ensures data is saved permanently even after crashes, using transaction logs and backups. Example: If a server crashes mid-transaction, the DBMS rolls back incomplete changes on restart so data remains consistent and nothing is half-written.

DBMS uses indexing, caching and query optimization to serve data fast even at scale. Example: An index on user_id lets the DBMS find a specific user record in milliseconds from a table with millions of rows, instead of scanning every row.

Relational databases store data in tables with rows and columns. Relationships are defined with keys. Example: A school database has a Students table and a Grades table linked by student_id — each grade row knows which student it belongs to.

Non-relational databases store data as documents, key-value pairs, graphs or time-series without a fixed table schema. Example: MongoDB stores a user profile as a JSON document with nested address and preferences fields — no need to split into multiple tables.

Use relational DBMS when data is structured and relationships matter (finance, HR). Use non-relational when data is flexible or large-scale unstructured (social media, IoT). Example: An online shop uses PostgreSQL for orders but Redis for caching session data.

Oracle Database is an enterprise-grade relational DBMS known for reliability and advanced features. Example: Large banks and telcos use Oracle for mission-critical transaction processing because it handles millions of transactions per day with high availability.

MySQL is an open-source relational DBMS widely used in web applications. Example: WordPress uses MySQL to store blog posts, comments and user accounts. It is known for ease of setup and strong community support.

PostgreSQL is an open-source, highly standards-compliant DBMS with advanced features like JSON support and full-text search. Example: Instagram uses PostgreSQL for its user data, leveraging its reliability and support for complex queries.

Microsoft SQL Server is a relational DBMS tightly integrated with Windows and Microsoft tools. Example: Many enterprise apps using .NET and Excel reports connect to SQL Server for data storage and business intelligence.
SQLite is a lightweight, file-based relational DBMS embedded directly in apps — no separate server needed. Example: Mobile apps like WhatsApp use SQLite to store chat history locally on the device.
IBM DB2 is an enterprise relational DBMS optimized for mainframes and large-scale analytics. Example: Airlines and insurance companies use DB2 for high-volume transaction systems that run 24/7.

MongoDB stores data as flexible JSON-like documents. Great for hierarchical or variable-structure data. Example: A content management system stores articles with varying fields (some with images, some with videos) as MongoDB documents without schema changes.

Cassandra is a distributed NoSQL database designed for massive scale and high write throughput. Example: Netflix uses Cassandra to store user viewing history across millions of users with no single point of failure.

Redis is an in-memory key-value store used mainly for caching and real-time data. Example: An e-commerce site uses Redis to cache product listings so pages load instantly without hitting the main database on every page view.

Amazon DynamoDB is a fully managed NoSQL key-value database on AWS that scales automatically. Example: Amazon uses DynamoDB for shopping cart data — it handles millions of concurrent reads and writes with single-digit millisecond latency.

CouchDB is a document-oriented database using HTTP/REST APIs and JSON documents with built-in replication. Example: An offline-capable mobile app uses CouchDB to sync data between device and server when connectivity is restored.

Elasticsearch is a distributed search and analytics engine built on Lucene. Example: A job portal uses Elasticsearch to provide instant full-text search across millions of job listings, returning ranked results in milliseconds.

SQL is a domain-specific language for managing relational databases. It covers data definition, manipulation and control. Example: SQL lets a data analyst retrieve last month's revenue or a developer create a new users table — all using the same language and database connection.

In SQL, you describe the desired result, not the step-by-step procedure to compute it. The DBMS chooses the best execution plan. Example: SELECT * FROM orders WHERE total > 500 tells SQL 'give me big orders' — you do not write loops or file-reading code.

CRUD stands for Create, Read, Update, Delete — the four fundamental data operations. SQL covers all: INSERT (create), SELECT (read), UPDATE (update), DELETE (delete). Example: An e-commerce app uses INSERT when a user places an order, SELECT to show order history, UPDATE to change address and DELETE to cancel an order.

The Read/Retrieve operation in SQL is performed with SELECT. It fetches rows from one or more tables based on conditions. Example: SELECT product_name, price FROM products WHERE stock > 0 retrieves all in-stock products for display in a web store.

The Update operation uses UPDATE ... SET ... WHERE. It modifies existing rows. Example: UPDATE employees SET salary = salary * 1.05 WHERE department = 'Engineering' gives all engineers a 5% raise in one statement.

The Delete operation removes rows using DELETE FROM ... WHERE. Without WHERE, all rows are deleted. Example: DELETE FROM sessions WHERE expires_at < NOW() cleans up expired login sessions from the sessions table.

INT (or INTEGER) stores whole numbers without decimals. Example: An orders table uses INT for quantity (you cannot order 2.5 items). Typical range: -2 billion to +2 billion. Use BIGINT for larger numbers like social media user counts.

FLOAT (or REAL, DOUBLE) stores numbers with decimal places. Example: A products table uses FLOAT for price so 19.99 is stored accurately. Be aware of floating-point precision issues for financial data — use DECIMAL instead.

VARCHAR(n) stores variable-length strings up to n characters. It uses only as much space as the actual string. Example: VARCHAR(100) for an email column stores 'a@b.com' using 7 bytes, not 100, making it space-efficient for variable-length names and emails.

TEXT stores strings of unlimited or very large length. Example: A blog posts table uses TEXT for the article body because articles can be thousands of words — far longer than what a VARCHAR(255) could hold.

DATE stores calendar dates in YYYY-MM-DD format without time. Example: A bookings table stores check_in_date as DATE so you can filter bookings with WHERE check_in_date BETWEEN '2024-06-01' AND '2024-06-30'.

TIME stores time of day in HH:MM:SS format without a date. Example: A restaurant reservation system stores reservation_time as TIME so queries like WHERE reservation_time BETWEEN '18:00' AND '21:00' find evening bookings.

DATETIME (or TIMESTAMP) stores both date and time. Example: An orders table uses DATETIME for created_at to record when each order was placed, enabling analyses like orders per hour and response-time calculations.

BOOLEAN stores true/false values. SQLite uses 1/0 integers. Example: A users table has is_active BOOLEAN; WHERE is_active = 1 fetches only active accounts. Some DBMS use TINYINT(1) as a stand-in for BOOLEAN.

Syntax: CREATE TABLE table_name (col1 datatype constraint, col2 datatype, ...);. Example: CREATE TABLE employees (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT NOT NULL, salary REAL, hire_date DATE) creates a table with four columns and a primary key constraint.

Each column has a datatype and optionally a DEFAULT value used when INSERT does not supply a value. Example: created_at DATETIME DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP automatically records the current date/time when a new row is inserted without specifying that column.

In SQLite, PRAGMA TABLE_INFO(table_name) lists each column's name, type, whether it allows NULL, default value and primary key flag. Example: PRAGMA TABLE_INFO(students) shows you that the id column is INTEGER PRIMARY KEY and name is TEXT NOT NULL.

Single row: INSERT INTO table (col1, col2) VALUES (v1, v2);. Multiple rows: add more comma-separated value groups. Example: INSERT INTO products (name, price) VALUES ('Pen', 10), ('Notebook', 50), ('Eraser', 5) inserts three products in one statement.

Values must match listed columns in number and order. If columns are omitted, values must match all columns in table-definition order. Example: INSERT INTO users VALUES (1, 'Alice', 'alice@mail.com') must match exactly (id, name, email) — mismatch causes an error.

Providing more or fewer values than columns causes an error. Example: INSERT INTO users (id, name) VALUES (1, 'Alice', 'extra') fails because 3 values are given for 2 columns. Always count columns and values carefully.

Inserting into a non-existent table gives 'no such table' error. Example: INSERT INTO usres VALUES (...) fails silently if you mistype the table name as 'usres' instead of 'users' — always verify the table name before running INSERT.

Wrapping the values list in extra parentheses is a common syntax mistake. Example: INSERT INTO t VALUES ((1, 'Alice')) uses double parentheses which is wrong; correct form is VALUES (1, 'Alice') with one level of parentheses.

Inserting a string into an INTEGER column may silently store 0 or raise an error depending on DBMS. Example: INSERT INTO scores (score) VALUES ('good') may store 0 in SQLite instead of rejecting it — always validate data types before inserting.

SELECT col1, col2 retrieves only the named columns (efficient). SELECT * retrieves all columns (convenient for exploration). Example: SELECT name, price FROM products is better than SELECT * in production because it avoids fetching unused columns and is faster on wide tables.
WHERE narrows the result to rows matching a condition. Example: SELECT * FROM orders WHERE status = 'pending' returns only unprocessed orders. Multiple conditions combine with AND/OR.

SQL keywords (SELECT, FROM, WHERE) are case-insensitive — SELECT and select are the same. String comparisons depend on DBMS collation settings. Best practice: write SQL keywords in UPPERCASE for readability. Example: SELECT name FROM Students is valid but SELECT name FROM students is clearer convention.

UPDATE table SET col = value updates all rows. Adding WHERE targets specific rows. Example: UPDATE products SET in_stock = 0 marks all products as out of stock; UPDATE products SET in_stock = 0 WHERE id = 42 only updates product 42.

DELETE FROM table removes all rows. DELETE FROM table WHERE condition removes only matching rows. Example: DELETE FROM expired_tokens WHERE created_at < '2024-01-01' removes old tokens; omitting WHERE deletes everything in the table.

DELETE removes rows but keeps the table structure. DROP TABLE removes the entire table including its structure and all data. Example: DELETE FROM temp_data clears rows for reuse; DROP TABLE temp_data removes the table entirely — requires creating it again to use.

Adds a new column to an existing table. Existing rows get NULL (or DEFAULT value) for the new column. Example: ALTER TABLE employees ADD COLUMN linkedin_url TEXT adds a LinkedIn profile field; all existing employee rows automatically get NULL for this new column.

Renames an existing column without changing its data or type. Example: ALTER TABLE customers RENAME COLUMN phone TO mobile_number renames the column; all existing data stays intact and queries must now use mobile_number.

Removes a column and all its data permanently. Example: ALTER TABLE users DROP COLUMN legacy_id removes an obsolete column; the data in that column is deleted and cannot be recovered — always back up before dropping.

When you add a column with DEFAULT, existing rows get that default instead of NULL. Example: ALTER TABLE products ADD COLUMN is_active INTEGER DEFAULT 1 sets all existing products as active (1) automatically without running a separate UPDATE statement.

= checks equality; <> (or !=) checks inequality; < and > check strict ordering; <= and >= are inclusive. Example: WHERE rating >= 4.5 fetches highly rated products; WHERE category <> 'Electronics' excludes electronics from the result set.

Comparison operators apply to text, numbers and dates. Example: WHERE brand = 'Nike' AND price <= 3000 finds Nike products costing at most 3000. Always put string values in quotes and numbers without quotes to avoid type mismatch errors.

WHERE category = 'Food' returns only food items; WHERE category <> 'Food' returns everything else. These simple filters demonstrate equality and inequality operators applied to a string column in a product catalog table.

Practice combining filters: WHERE rating > 4.5 AND price <= 1000 finds well-rated affordable products. WHERE brand = 'Puma' filters by brand. WHERE category <> 'Gadgets' excludes gadgets. Combine them with AND/OR for more targeted queries.

% matches any sequence of characters (including empty). _ matches exactly one character. Example: 'A%' matches 'Apple', 'App', 'A'; 'A_' matches 'An', 'At' but not 'Apple'. Combine them: '_at%' matches 'Catch', 'Batch', 'Batman'.

No wildcards = exact. 'word%' = starts with. '%word' = ends with. '%word%' = contains. Example: LIKE 'S%' finds all products starting with S; LIKE '%Shirt' finds products ending in Shirt; LIKE '%Slim%' finds products containing Slim.

Place LIKE in the WHERE clause with the pattern in single quotes. Example: WHERE product_name LIKE 'Bour%' matches 'Bourbon Biscuits'. LIKE is case-insensitive in many DBMS but wrap in LOWER() to ensure consistency: WHERE LOWER(name) LIKE 'bour%'.

LIKE 'Bourbon%' finds products starting with Bourbon. LIKE '%Smart%' finds products with Smart anywhere in the name (e.g., smartphone). LIKE '_____' (five underscores) finds brands with exactly 5 characters — useful for finding short brand codes.

WHERE category = 'Food' uses equality. WHERE name LIKE '%Cake%' finds all cake products. WHERE name LIKE '%T-Shirt' finds items ending in T-Shirt. These three patterns cover the most common filtering scenarios on string columns.

AND returns rows only where every condition is true. Adding more AND conditions narrows results. Example: WHERE brand = 'Samsung' AND category = 'Electronics' AND price < 20000 finds Samsung electronics under 20k — all three must hold simultaneously.

OR returns rows where at least one condition is true. It broadens the result. Example: WHERE brand = 'Apple' OR brand = 'Samsung' returns rows from either brand — useful when filtering across a set of acceptable values without using IN.

NOT inverts a condition — rows where the condition is false are returned. Example: WHERE NOT name LIKE '%Cake%' returns all products that do NOT contain Cake in their name, excluding baked goods from the result.

Combine all three: WHERE (brand = 'Nike' OR brand = 'Adidas') AND price <= 2000 AND NOT category = 'Accessories' finds Nike or Adidas products costing at most 2000 that are not accessories — a realistic e-commerce filter.

NOT binds tightest, then AND, then OR. Example: WHERE a OR b AND c is parsed as WHERE a OR (b AND c) — not (a OR b) AND c. This matters: brand='Puma' OR brand='Nike' AND rating>4 returns all Puma products (any rating) plus Nike products above 4 rating.

Use parentheses to make your intent explicit and override default precedence. Example: WHERE (brand='Puma' OR brand='Nike') AND rating>4 correctly returns both Puma and Nike products but only those above rating 4. Without parentheses the meaning changes.

This returns Redmi phones rated above 4 plus ALL OnePlus phones (any rating). The parentheses apply the AND only to Redmi. Without them, rating>4 would also apply to OnePlus, narrowing results more than intended.

Without parentheses, OR mixed with AND leads to unintended grouping. Example: WHERE a=1 OR b=2 AND c=3 actually means WHERE a=1 OR (b=2 AND c=3). If you intended (a=1 OR b=2) AND c=3, you must add parentheses — the result is completely different.

Real-world filtering: WHERE (brand IN ('Nike','Adidas') OR category='Sports') AND price BETWEEN 500 AND 3000 AND NOT name LIKE '%Kids%' finds adult sports brand products in a price range. Parentheses make the logic readable and correct.

Example: WHERE (brand='Levis' OR brand='Wrangler') AND NOT name LIKE '%Jeans%' finds non-Jeans items from those brands. Practice by adding AND price<2000 step by step, checking row counts after each addition to verify the filter behaves as expected.

IN checks if a column value matches any item in a list. It is equivalent to multiple OR conditions but cleaner. Example: WHERE country IN ('India', 'USA', 'UK') returns rows from any of the three countries — same as OR-ing three equality conditions.

BETWEEN v1 AND v2 is inclusive — rows where value equals v1 or v2 are included. Example: WHERE price BETWEEN 500 AND 1500 returns products costing from 500 to 1500 inclusive. Equivalent to WHERE price >= 500 AND price <= 1500.

BETWEEN requires the lower bound first. BETWEEN 1500 AND 500 returns no rows in most DBMS. Type mismatch: BETWEEN '2' AND '10' on strings gives wrong results due to lexicographic order ('9' > '10'). Always verify bounds are in correct order and types match.

Example: SELECT * FROM products WHERE rating BETWEEN 4.3 AND 4.8 fetches mid-to-high rated items. Combine with IN: WHERE rating BETWEEN 4.3 AND 4.8 AND brand IN ('Sony','Samsung') for targeted searches.

ORDER BY col ASC sorts low-to-high (default); DESC sorts high-to-low. Multiple columns: rows are sorted by the first column, then ties broken by the second. Example: ORDER BY rating DESC, price ASC shows best-rated products first; ties in rating are broken by lowest price.

If two products have rating 4.8, ORDER BY rating DESC, price ASC places the cheaper one first. Example: A product search result page uses this to show top-rated products, letting price break ties so users see better value items first.

SELECT DISTINCT removes duplicate rows from results. Often used to list unique values in a column. Example: SELECT DISTINCT category FROM products returns each category exactly once, useful for populating a filter dropdown in a shopping app.

SELECT * FROM products WHERE name LIKE '%Shirt%' ORDER BY rating DESC, price ASC lists shirts best-to-worst rated with price as tie-breaker. SELECT DISTINCT category FROM products lists unique categories. Both are common UI-building queries.

LIMIT n returns at most n rows. Useful for top-N queries and previews. Example: SELECT * FROM products ORDER BY rating DESC LIMIT 5 returns the 5 highest-rated products — perfect for a 'Top 5 Picks' widget on a homepage.

OFFSET n skips the first n rows before returning results. Always combine with ORDER BY for consistent pagination. Example: LIMIT 10 OFFSET 30 skips the first 30 rows and returns rows 31–40, which is page 4 when showing 10 items per page.

In SQLite, OFFSET must follow LIMIT — you cannot use OFFSET without LIMIT. Example: SELECT * FROM products OFFSET 5 errors in SQLite; correct form is SELECT * FROM products LIMIT -1 OFFSET 5 (LIMIT -1 means no cap) to skip first 5 rows.

Top 2 by rating: SELECT * FROM products ORDER BY rating DESC LIMIT 2. Rows 7–11: SELECT * FROM products ORDER BY id LIMIT 5 OFFSET 6. Always specify ORDER BY with pagination to ensure consistent results across pages.

Using spread to copy an array creates a new array with the same elements but a different reference, so changes to the copy don't affect the original — this is a shallow copy. Example: const a = [1,2,3]; const b = [...a]; b is a new independent array. Useful whenever you need to avoid mutating original data.

Spread lets you merge two or more arrays into one in a readable way. Example: const merged = [...arr1, ...arr2] combines both arrays in order. Far cleaner than arr1.concat(arr2) and lets you insert extra elements inline at any position.

Spread copies all own enumerable properties of an object into a new object. Example: const clone = {...original} — clone has the same keys and values but is a separate object. It is a shallow copy, so nested objects are still shared by reference.

You can merge two objects with spread. If both have the same key, the later one wins. Example: const merged = {...defaults, ...overrides} — any key in overrides replaces the same key from defaults. Great for building config objects.

Spread can expand an array as separate arguments to a function. Example: Math.max(...[3, 1, 4]) is equivalent to Math.max(3, 1, 4). Very handy when your data is already in an array but the function expects individual arguments.

When you prefix the last function parameter with ..., all extra arguments passed to that function are collected into a single array. Example: function log(level, ...messages) — calling log('info', 'a', 'b') gives messages = ['a', 'b']. Useful for variadic functions that accept any number of arguments.

In array destructuring, the rest syntax grabs all remaining elements after the named ones. Example: const [first, ...rest] = [1, 2, 3, 4] gives first = 1 and rest = [2, 3, 4]. Good for splitting the head from the tail of a list.

In object destructuring, rest collects all remaining properties that were not explicitly named. Example: const {a, ...others} = {a: 1, b: 2, c: 3} gives a = 1 and others = {b: 2, c: 3}. Useful for excluding specific keys from an object without mutation.

The rest parameter must always be the final parameter in a function definition. Placing it before other parameters causes a SyntaxError. Example: function fn(a, b, ...rest) is valid; function fn(...rest, a) is not. This rule lets JS know exactly which arguments to pack into the rest array.

Set defaults using = in the parameter list. Example: function multiply(a, b = 1) { return a * b; } — multiply(5) returns 5 because b defaults to 1. You can use any expression, even a function call, as the default value.

If an argument is not passed or is explicitly undefined, the default kicks in. Passing null does NOT trigger the default — null is a valid value. Example: greet(undefined) uses the default; greet(null) uses null. Knowing this distinction prevents subtle bugs.

Inside a template literal, wrap any variable or expression in ${} and it gets evaluated and converted to a string inline. Example: const greeting = `Hi ${firstName} ${lastName}!` — no + operators needed. Keeps string construction readable and much less error-prone.

Template literals preserve newlines so you can span them across multiple lines without escape characters like \n. Example: a multi-line HTML snippet can be written naturally across lines in a backtick string. Ideal for SQL queries or HTML templates inside JS.

You can put any valid JS expression inside ${}, not just variables. Example: `Total: ${price * qty}` or `${isLoggedIn ? 'Welcome' : 'Log in'}`. This keeps logic and text together cleanly without breaking the string into separate pieces.

MongoDB is the NoSQL database; Express is the web framework on Node for routing; React is the front-end UI library; Node.js is the server-side JS runtime. Together they let one team write the entire stack in JavaScript. Example: a blog with a React UI, Express REST API, and MongoDB posts collection.

Because all four layers use JavaScript (and JSON), data flows from DB to API to UI with minimal transformation. This reduces context-switching and speeds up development. Example: a startup can ship a full MVP faster because one codebase handles everything from DB queries to UI rendering.

Before Node, JavaScript only ran in browsers. Node wraps Chrome's V8 engine so JS runs on your OS just like Python or Java. Example: node app.js runs a JS script from the terminal. This enables server-side logic, file I/O, and system scripting all in JavaScript.

Node runs on Windows, macOS, and Linux without code changes. It ships with npm, which hosts over a million open-source packages. Being open source means a huge community maintains and improves it. Example: npm install express takes one command to add a full web framework.

The REPL is an interactive terminal where you type JS and see results immediately. Start it with just node. It reads input, evaluates it, prints the result, and loops. Example: typing 2 + 2 and pressing Enter instantly shows 4. Great for quick tests without creating a file.

To run a complete script, save it as a .js file and run node filename.js from your terminal. Always save the file before running or you will execute old code. Example: node greet.js runs the saved greet.js script and prints its output to the terminal.

By default a file's contents are private. To share something, assign it to module.exports. Example: module.exports = { add: (a,b) => a+b }; — any other file can import and use the add function. This keeps codebases organized and prevents naming collisions.

Use require('./path/to/file') to import another local module. The path is relative to the current file and you can omit the .js extension. Example: const utils = require('./utils') loads the exports of utils.js. require() returns whatever module.exports was set to.

Setting module.exports to a value replaces the entire export for that file. require() then returns exactly that value. Example: module.exports = function add(a,b){return a+b;} — the importing file gets the add function directly without any extra property access.

exports is a shorthand reference to module.exports. Attach properties to it for named exports. Example: exports.add = (a,b)=>a+b; exports.subtract = (a,b)=>a-b; — importers can destructure: const {add, subtract} = require('./math').

When a module exports an object, you can destructure on import. Example: const {add, multiply} = require('./math') pulls out only the functions needed. Equivalent to importing the whole object and accessing properties, but more readable.

Use export default for a single main export and export const name = ... for named ones. import myDefault from './file' gets the default; import {name} from './file' gets a named export. Example: export default class User {} and import User from './user.mjs' on the other side.

A default export can be imported with any name you choose. Named exports must match exactly (or be aliased with as). Example: import anything from './mod' works for default; import {exact as alias} from './mod' for named. Both can be combined in one import statement.

In Node.js, ES6 module files typically need a .mjs extension, or package.json must have "type": "module". Without this, Node treats files as CommonJS and import/export will error. Example: rename app.js to app.mjs and use import/export freely. In browsers, add type="module" to script tags.

path handles file path manipulation. fs handles reading and writing files. url parses and builds URLs. All are part of Node. Example: const fs = require('fs'); fs.readFileSync('data.txt', 'utf8') reads a file synchronously with no install needed.

Import a core module by passing its name (not a path) to require(). Example: const path = require('path') — note no ./ prefix. Node finds it automatically. This differs from local modules which need a relative path starting with ./

Key methods: path.join() builds a path from segments; path.resolve() turns relative paths into absolute; path.basename() extracts the filename; path.dirname() gets the folder. Example: path.basename('/users/app/index.js') returns 'index.js'. Prevents bugs from manual string concatenation of paths.

path.join(...segments) joins path parts with the correct OS separator, removes redundant slashes, and handles .. correctly. Example: path.join(__dirname, 'data', 'users.json') builds a safe absolute path to users.json relative to the current file, working on both Mac and Windows.

A package is a folder with a package.json describing it and one or more JS files. The package.json defines name, version, entry point, and dependencies. Example: the express package is a folder with many module files that together provide a web framework.

npmjs.com is the public registry where developers publish packages. The npm CLI lets you search, install, and manage them. Example: npm search date finds date-related packages. Millions of packages cover everything from utility functions to full frameworks.

npm init -y creates a package.json with default values instantly. npm install <pkg> --save installs a package and records it as a dependency. Example: npm install axios --save adds axios so others can reproduce your environment with a single npm install.

First create a project directory with mkdir project-name, then navigate into it with cd project-name. All npm commands and file operations will now be scoped to this folder. Example: mkdir blog-app && cd blog-app sets up an isolated workspace for your new project.

npm init -y auto-generates a package.json with sensible defaults (name from folder name, version 1.0.0) without asking questions. This file tracks your project metadata and dependencies. Example: after running it, package.json is ready for you to add scripts and install packages.

Run npm install date-fns --save to download and record it as a dependency. npm creates a node_modules folder and saves the entry in package.json. Example: after install, import {format} from 'date-fns' and call format(new Date(), 'yyyy-MM-dd') to get a formatted date string.

addDays(date, n) returns a new Date n days after the input date. Important: JavaScript's built-in Date months are 0-indexed (0 = January). Example: addDays(new Date(2024, 0, 1), 5) returns Jan 6 2024 (month 0 = January). date-fns works with standard Date objects, so this note applies throughout.

The ternary has three parts: a condition (truthy/falsy check), the value returned if true, and the value returned if false. Example: const discount = isMember ? 0.2 : 0 gives 20% for members, 0 for others. It is an expression, so it can live inside template literals or function calls directly.

Use ternary when assigning one of two values based on a condition. Avoid nesting ternaries as they become unreadable quickly. Example: const color = score > 90 ? 'green' : 'red' is clean and clear. For complex branching with more than two cases, a regular if/else or switch is better.

switch(expression) compares the expression to each case using strict equality (===). When a match is found, that block runs. A default case acts like else — it runs if no case matches. Example: switch(fruit){ case 'apple': console.log('A'); break; default: console.log('other'); }

If you omit break after a case, execution continues (falls through) into the next case block even if the next case doesn't match. This can be a bug or intentional. Example: case 'Mon': case 'Tue': doWeekday(); break; — both Monday and Tuesday run doWeekday() because Mon falls through to Tue.

Arrow functions drop the function keyword and use =>. Example: const add = (a, b) => a + b is equivalent to const add = function(a,b){ return a+b; }. Commonly used as callbacks: [1,2,3].map(n => n * 2).

If the function body is a single expression, omit curly braces and the return keyword — the expression is returned automatically. Example: const square = x => x * x implicitly returns x * x. This only works for single expressions, not multi-statement bodies.

When an arrow function has exactly one parameter, parentheses are optional. Example: const double = n => n * 2 is valid. With zero or two or more parameters, parentheses are required: () => 42 or (a, b) => a + b.

When there are no parameters, you must include empty parentheses before =>. Example: const greet = () => 'Hello!' — you cannot omit the (). This is the one form of parentheses that is always required in arrow functions.

If an arrow function implicitly returns an object literal, wrap it in parentheses to avoid it being parsed as a code block. Example: const makeObj = id => ({ id }) — without the outer (), the {} is treated as a function body, not an object. This is a very common beginner mistake.

When a function is called as a method of an object (obj.method()), this refers to that object. Example: const user = { name: 'Alice', greet(){ return this.name; } } — calling user.greet() returns 'Alice' because this is the user object at the time of the call.

A regular function called without an object context has this set to the global object (window in browsers, global in Node) in non-strict mode. In strict mode, this is undefined. Example: function show(){ console.log(this); } — in the browser this logs the window object.

Arrow functions do not have their own this. They capture this from the surrounding scope when they are defined. Example: in a method that uses setInterval with an arrow callback, this inside the callback still refers to the parent object, not the global object.

When called with new, JS creates a new empty object, sets this to that object, runs the function body, and returns this. Example: function Car(brand){ this.brand = brand; } — new Car('Toyota') creates {brand:'Toyota'} because this was the new instance inside the constructor.

Arrow functions are ideal as event or timer callbacks when you need this to refer to the enclosing object. Example: class Timer { start(){ setInterval(() => { this.tick(); }, 1000); } } — the arrow preserves this as the Timer instance rather than the global object.

Numbers, strings, booleans, undefined, null, and symbols are primitives. They cannot be altered in place. Example: 'hello'.toUpperCase() does not change the original string — it returns a new string 'HELLO'. To change a primitive you reassign the variable, you do not modify the value itself.

Strings in JavaScript are immutable. Methods like .slice() or .replace() always return new strings without touching the original. Example: const original = 'hello'; const upper = original.toUpperCase(); — original is still 'hello'; upper is the new string 'HELLO'.

Booleans (true/false) are primitive and immutable. You cannot mutate a boolean value — you can only reassign a variable. Example: let flag = true; flag = false; — this reassigns the variable, it does not modify the original true value.

Symbols are unique, immutable primitive values used as object property keys. Each Symbol() call creates a totally unique value. Example: const id = Symbol('id') — even a second Symbol('id') is not equal to the first. Useful for preventing property name collisions on objects.

undefined is the default value JS gives to uninitialized variables. It is a primitive and immutable. Example: let x; console.log(x) outputs undefined. You cannot modify undefined itself — you can only reassign the variable to something else.

Objects are mutable — you can add, change, or delete their properties after creation. Example: const user = {name: 'Alice'}; user.name = 'Bob'; — this modifies the same object. Even declared with const, the object's properties can still be changed.

Arrays are objects and are mutable. Methods like push(), pop(), and splice() change the array in place. Example: const nums = [1,2,3]; nums.push(4); — nums is now [1,2,3,4]. const only prevents reassigning the variable, not mutating the array's contents.

Functions are objects in JS and can have properties added to them at any time. Example: function greet(){} greet.language = 'English'; — this works because functions are mutable objects. You can add and change function properties just like regular object properties.

When you assign an object to another variable, both point to the same memory location. Changing through one variable affects the other. Example: const a = {x:1}; const b = a; b.x = 99; — a.x is now also 99. Use spread or Object.assign to make an independent copy.

Primitives are copied by value: let a=5; let b=a; b=10; — a is still 5. Objects are copied by reference: let o={v:5}; let p=o; p.v=10; — o.v is now 10. This distinction is fundamental and a frequent source of bugs in JS applications.

let declares a block-scoped variable that can be initialized later and reassigned freely. Example: let count = 0; count = count + 1; is valid. Scoped to the nearest enclosing block, so it does not accidentally leak into outer scopes the way var does.

const declares a block-scoped variable that must be initialized at declaration and cannot be reassigned. Example: const PI = 3.14; PI = 3 throws a TypeError. However, if const holds an object or array, its contents can still be mutated — const prevents rebinding, not mutation.

var is the old way to declare variables. It is function-scoped (not block-scoped) and hoisted to the top of its function. Example: using var inside an if block makes it accessible outside the block within the same function. This leads to surprising bugs and is why let/const are preferred.

Variables declared with let can be reassigned at any time after declaration. Example: let score = 10; score = 20; is perfectly valid. This makes let the right choice for counters, accumulators, or any value that changes over time.

Block scope means a variable is only accessible within the {} block where it was declared. Example: if(true){ let x = 5; } console.log(x); throws ReferenceError because x only existed inside the if block. This scoping makes code predictable.

Unlike let, const must be assigned a value at declaration time. Writing const x; without a value causes a SyntaxError immediately. Example: const name = 'Alice'; is valid; const name; is not. This enforces that constants always have a known value from the start.

Once a const variable is set, you cannot use = to point it at a new value. Example: const url = 'https://example.com'; url = 'https://other.com'; throws TypeError. The binding (the variable-to-value link) is frozen, though the value may be mutable if it is an object.

const only prevents reassignment of the variable, not changes to the value it holds. Example: const user = {name:'Alice'}; user.name = 'Bob'; is perfectly valid — you changed a property of the object, not the variable itself. To fully freeze an object, use Object.freeze().

var is hoisted to the top of its enclosing function and initialized as undefined. This means you can reference a var variable before its declaration line without an error (though its value is undefined). Example: console.log(x); var x = 5; logs undefined, not a ReferenceError.

A const array or object's internal contents are freely mutable. Example: const arr = [1,2]; arr.push(3); is valid. What const prevents is arr = [4,5] (rebinding the variable). This is a common source of confusion for beginners expecting const to freeze everything.

The defining trait of a factory function is that it always returns a new object literal. Each call produces an independent object. Example: function createPoint(x,y){ return {x, y}; } — createPoint(1,2) and createPoint(3,4) give two separate objects with no class or new required.

Factory functions follow regular function naming — camelCase starting with a lowercase letter. This distinguishes them from constructor functions (PascalCase). Example: createUser, makeProduct, buildConfig all signal factory functions. The naming convention is a social contract in JS, not enforced by the language.

Inside the returned object, use shorthand syntax: if the variable name matches the property name, write it once. Methods can also use shorthand. Example: function createUser(name){ return { name, greet(){ return 'Hi ' + this.name; } }; } — name:name can just be written as name.

Calling a constructor with new triggers special behavior: a new empty object is created, this is set to it, the function body runs, and the new object is returned implicitly. Example: function Dog(name){ this.name = name; } const d = new Dog('Rex'); — d is {name:'Rex'}. Forgetting new makes this the global object and breaks everything.

Constructor functions use PascalCase to signal they must be called with new. Example: Person, Car, ProductItem — these look different from regular functions on purpose. The capitalization warns developers: use new with this function or you will introduce a hard-to-find bug.

When new runs a constructor: 1) a new object is created, 2) this points to that object, 3) properties set on this become instance properties, 4) the new object is returned automatically (no return statement needed). Example: function Box(size){ this.size=size; } — new Box(10) returns {size:10} implicitly.

Factories use camelCase and are called like regular functions. Constructors use PascalCase and require new. Example: const car = createCar('Toyota') (factory) vs const car = new Car('Toyota') (constructor). Forgetting new on a constructor is a very common bug — the naming convention exists to prevent this.

Factories must explicitly return the object with return { ... }. Constructors return the new this object implicitly — no return statement needed. Example: factory: return {name}; — constructor: just this.name = name; and new handles the rest. Both patterns are valid; factories tend to be simpler and more predictable.

Every function has a name property (the declared name as a string) and a length property (number of formal parameters, excluding rest). Example: const fn = function multiply(a, b){}; fn.name gives 'multiply' and fn.length gives 2. Useful for debugging and building higher-order utility functions.

call(thisArg, arg1, arg2) calls the function with a specific this. apply(thisArg, [args]) is the same but takes an array of args. bind(thisArg) returns a new function with this permanently locked. toString() returns the function source as a string. Example: greet.call(user) makes this = user inside greet.

Using typeof on a function returns 'function', not 'object' (even though functions are objects). Example: typeof function(){} === 'function' is true. This makes it easy to check if a value is callable before invoking it, e.g., if(typeof callback === 'function') callback();

Four ways to create a Date: new Date() (current moment), new Date(ms) (milliseconds since epoch), new Date('2024-01-15') (ISO string), new Date(2024, 0, 15) (year, 0-indexed month, day). Example: new Date(0) gives Jan 1 1970 UTC — the Unix epoch start.

JS Date auto-corrects invalid values. Example: new Date(2024, 0, 32) becomes Feb 1 2024 because January only has 31 days. This is handy for date arithmetic (add days by setting a larger day value) but can confuse you if you are not expecting automatic correction.

Getters read components: getFullYear() (4-digit year), getMonth() (0-11), getDate() (1-31). Setters change them: setFullYear(2025), setMonth(11), setDate(1). Example: const d = new Date(); d.getMonth() returns 0 for January. Always remember months are 0-indexed in all native Date methods.

Declare a class with class ClassName { ... }. Define methods directly inside the body without the function keyword and without commas between them. Example: class Circle { constructor(r){ this.r=r; } area(){ return Math.PI*this.r**2; } } — clean compared to prototype-based syntax.

The constructor is a special method called automatically when you use new ClassName(). It sets up the initial state of the new instance using this. Example: class User { constructor(name, age){ this.name=name; this.age=age; } } — new User('Alice',25) creates {name:'Alice',age:25}.

Even though class looks like a new construct, under the hood it is syntactic sugar over constructor functions. Example: typeof class Car {} returns 'function'. Classes are callable with new, inherit via prototype, and behave exactly like constructor functions internally.

The new keyword triggers the constructor, creates a new object, and sets this inside the constructor to that new object. The result is returned automatically. Example: const p = new Product('Phone', 999); — p is now {name:'Phone', price:999} with all Product methods available.

One class can produce unlimited instances, each independent. Example: const u1 = new User('Alice'); const u2 = new User('Bob'); — u1 and u2 are separate objects. Changing u1.name does not affect u2.name. This is the main purpose of classes: reusable object blueprints.

Properties defined with this.prop in the constructor belong to each instance. Methods in the class body are shared on the prototype. Example: u1.greet() works because greet is on User.prototype, shared by all instances. But u1.name is unique to u1 and lives on the instance itself.

class Child extends Parent sets up Child to inherit all methods and properties from Parent. Instances of Child can call Parent methods without redefining them. Example: class ElectricCar extends Car { ... } — an ElectricCar instance can call any Car method automatically through the prototype chain.

In a subclass constructor, super() must be called before using this — it runs the parent class constructor. Example: class Cat extends Animal { constructor(name, indoor){ super(name); this.indoor=indoor; } } — super(name) sets this.name from Animal, then this.indoor is added by Cat.

A subclass can define a method with the same name as the parent to override it. The subclass version runs instead. Example: Animal has speak(){ return 'Some sound'; }; Dog overrides with speak(){ return 'Woof'; }. Use super.speak() inside the override to also call the parent version if needed.

When you call a method on an instance, this inside that method is the instance itself. Example: class Counter { count=0; increment(){ this.count++; } } — calling c.increment() increments c.count because this is c. This is why class methods can encapsulate behavior for each object.

In a subclass constructor, this is not accessible until super() has been called. Using this before super() throws a ReferenceError. Example: class Sub extends Base { constructor(){ this.x=1; super(); } } throws. Move super() above any this usage to fix the error.

Methods can return this to enable method chaining — calling multiple methods in sequence on the same object. Example: class Builder { setName(n){ this.name=n; return this; } setAge(a){ this.age=a; return this; } } — new Builder().setName('Ali').setAge(30) works because each method returns the same instance.

In synchronous code, each statement completes before the next starts. The call stack processes one thing at a time. Example: console.log('A'); console.log('B'); always prints A then B in order. Simple and predictable, but slow if any step takes a long time.

Async operations like network requests start in the background and notify your code when done, so the program keeps running. Example: fetch('https://api.example.com/data') begins the request immediately but doesn't freeze the page — other JS continues running while waiting for the response.

alert() is synchronous — it blocks the entire page until the user clicks OK. fetch() is asynchronous — it fires the request and immediately returns a Promise, letting the page stay responsive. This contrast shows exactly why async patterns like promises and async/await are essential for web apps.

A Promise is like an IOU: it says 'I will give you a value eventually, but not right now.' It is an object with a state (pending, fulfilled, rejected) and an associated value. Example: const p = fetch(url) gives you a Promise object immediately even though the data hasn't arrived yet.

fetch(url) is the most common way to get a Promise. It makes an HTTP GET request and returns a Promise that fulfills with a Response object. Example: fetch('https://api.github.com/users/octocat') returns a Promise. Once resolved, the Response lets you call .json() to get the actual data.

If you console.log a Promise before it resolves, you see Promise { <pending> }. After it resolves, you might see Promise { <fulfilled>: value }. You cannot access the value directly from the Promise object — you must use .then() to receive it when it becomes available.

A Promise starts in the pending state immediately after creation and stays there until the async operation completes. Example: the moment you call fetch(), the returned Promise is pending — the request is in flight but hasn't resolved yet. Your code continues running in the meantime.

A Promise is fulfilled when the async operation succeeds and produces a value. The .then() handler receives this value. Example: a fetch() to a valid URL fulfills with a Response object. You then call response.json() to get the parsed data from the response body.

A Promise is rejected when the async operation fails. The reason (usually an Error) is passed to .catch(). Example: fetch('https://not-a-real-url') rejects with TypeError: Failed to fetch. Always add .catch() to handle errors and prevent unhandled rejection warnings.

.then(onFulfilled) registers a callback that runs when the Promise fulfills. The callback receives the resolved value. Example: fetch(url).then(response => { console.log(response.status); }) — response is the fulfilled value. You can chain multiple .then() calls to process data step by step.

.catch(onRejected) registers a callback that runs if the Promise rejects. It receives the rejection reason (usually an Error). Example: fetch(badUrl).catch(err => console.log('Error:', err.message)) — handles network errors gracefully. Without .catch(), unhandled rejections may silently crash your app.

Whatever you return from a .then() callback becomes the resolved value of the next .then() in the chain. Example: fetch(url).then(res => res.json()).then(data => data.name) — the second .then gets the parsed JSON object. This is how you build clean data pipelines with promises.

Each .then() returns a new Promise, so you keep chaining. One .catch() at the end catches any error from any step. Example: fetchUser(id).then(user => fetchPosts(user.id)).then(posts => display(posts)).catch(err => alert(err.message)) — three async steps with a single shared error handler.

If a .then() callback returns a Promise, the next .then() waits for that Promise to resolve before it runs. This is how you sequence async operations without nesting. Example: .then(res => res.json()) returns a new Promise for the parsed body, and the next .then gets the actual JS object.

This two-step pattern is the standard fetch usage: first .then converts the Response to JSON (itself async), the second .then works with the actual data. Example: fetch('/api/users').then(r => r.json()).then(users => renderList(users)) is the bread and butter of browser data fetching.

After fetch resolves, you get a Response object, not the data. Call response.json() to parse the body as JSON — this returns another Promise. Example: fetch('/api/data').then(r => r.json()).then(data => console.log(data)) — two .then() calls are the standard pattern to get usable data.

If the URL is invalid or the server is unreachable, fetch rejects with a TypeError. Always add .catch() to handle this. Example: fetch('https://invalid-url').catch(e => console.log(e.message)) logs 'Failed to fetch'. Without catch, the error goes unhandled and may silently break your app.

Testing fetch with a valid URL shows a fulfilled Promise; an invalid URL shows rejection with TypeError. Also test with a 404 URL — fetch still fulfills (HTTP error is not a network error) but response.ok is false. Example: always check response.ok before calling .json() to handle HTTP errors properly.

Callbacks are functions passed as arguments to be called later. setTimeout(fn, ms) is a classic example — it calls fn after ms milliseconds. Example: setTimeout(() => console.log('Done'), 1000) — the arrow function is the callback. Works but gets messy with deeply nested async operations.

setInterval(fn, ms) repeatedly calls fn every ms milliseconds until cleared with clearInterval(). Example: const id = setInterval(() => console.log('tick'), 500) prints 'tick' every half second. Call clearInterval(id) to stop it. Both setTimeout and setInterval are callback-based timer APIs.

fetch() is the flagship Promise-based API. It returns a Promise for an HTTP response, making async code cleaner with .then() chaining instead of nested callbacks. Example: fetch('/api').then(r=>r.json()).then(data=>render(data)) is much more readable than equivalent callback-based code.

The Promise constructor lets you create custom Promises: new Promise((resolve, reject) => { ... }). Call resolve(value) on success or reject(reason) on failure. Example: const wait = ms => new Promise(res => setTimeout(res, ms)) creates a Promise-based delay utility usable with .then() or await.

new Promise(executorFn) takes a function that runs immediately with two arguments: resolve and reject. The executor contains the async logic. Example: new Promise((resolve, reject) => { setTimeout(() => resolve('hello'), 1000); }) creates a Promise that fulfills with 'hello' after one second.

resolve(value) transitions the Promise to fulfilled state with value as the result. reject(reason) transitions it to rejected state. Only the first call has any effect — calling both does nothing after the first. Example: resolve('data') means .then(data => ...) will receive 'data'.

Return a new Promise from a function to make it async-capable. Example: function delay(ms){ return new Promise(res => setTimeout(res, ms)); } — calling delay(500).then(() => console.log('waited')) pauses 500ms before logging. This pattern is how you promisify callback-based APIs.

.then(onFulfilled) runs when the Promise resolves. The resolved value is passed to the callback. Example: fetchData().then(data => console.log(data.name)) — when fetchData resolves, the callback receives the data. .then always returns a new Promise, making chaining straightforward.

.catch(onRejected) runs when any Promise in the chain rejects. It receives the rejection reason (usually an Error). Example: fetchData().then(process).catch(err => showErrorMessage(err.message)) — a single catch at the end handles errors from any step in the chain above it.

Whatever value you pass to resolve() is received by .then(). Whatever you pass to reject() is received by .catch(). Example: resolve({user:'Alice'}) means .then(result => result.user) gives 'Alice'. reject(new Error('Not found')) means .catch(e => e.message) gives 'Not found'.

async before a function makes it return a Promise. await before a Promise expression pauses the async function until the Promise settles, then gives you the resolved value. Example: async function getUser(){ const res = await fetch('/user'); return res.json(); } — reads like synchronous code.

Wrap await calls in try/catch to handle rejections cleanly instead of chaining .catch(). Example: async function load(){ try{ const data = await fetchData(); render(data); } catch(err){ showError(err); } } — if fetchData rejects, the catch block runs. Far cleaner than nested .catch() chains.

Even if you return a plain value from an async function, JS wraps it in a resolved Promise automatically. Example: async function hi(){ return 'Hello'; } — hi() returns a Promise that fulfills with 'Hello'. You can call hi().then(msg => console.log(msg)) even though the body looks synchronous.

toUpperCase() converts all characters to uppercase. Example: 'hello'.toUpperCase() returns 'HELLO'. Useful for case-insensitive comparisons: if(input.toUpperCase() === 'YES'). Strings are immutable — this returns a new string without touching the original.

toLowerCase() converts all characters to lowercase. Example: 'Hello World'.toLowerCase() returns 'hello world'. Commonly used when normalizing user input for comparison or storage, such as email addresses or usernames.

trim() removes whitespace from both ends of a string. Example: '  hello  '.trim() returns 'hello'. Essential for cleaning form input. Also available as trimStart() and trimEnd() for one-sided trimming.

includes(substring) returns true if the string contains the given substring. Example: 'JavaScript is fun'.includes('fun') returns true. Case-sensitive. Great for simple search checks without needing regex.
startsWith(str) returns true if the string begins with str. Example: 'hello world'.startsWith('hello') returns true. Useful for checking URL prefixes, file extensions, or command syntax.
endsWith(str) returns true if the string ends with str. Example: 'photo.jpg'.endsWith('.jpg') returns true. Common use case: validating file extensions or checking sentence-ending punctuation.

indexOf(substr) returns the index of the first occurrence of substr, or -1 if not found. Example: 'hello'.indexOf('l') returns 2. Useful for finding the position of a character to build slicing or splitting logic around it.

slice(start, end) extracts a portion of a string from start up to (but not including) end. Negative indices count from the end. Example: 'hello world'.slice(0, 5) returns 'hello'. Does not modify the original string.

substring(start, end) is similar to slice but does not support negative indices. Example: 'JavaScript'.substring(0, 4) returns 'Java'. For most use cases, slice is preferred because of its negative-index support and more consistent behavior.

split(delimiter) splits a string into an array of substrings. Example: 'a,b,c'.split(',') returns ['a','b','c']. Pass '' to split every character. Useful for parsing CSV-style data or breaking a sentence into words.

replace(search, replacement) returns a new string with the first match replaced. Use a regex with the g flag to replace all occurrences. Example: 'foo foo'.replace(/foo/g,'bar') returns 'bar bar'. The original string is unchanged.

concat(str1, str2, ...) joins strings together. Example: 'Hello'.concat(' ', 'World') returns 'Hello World'. In practice, template literals and the + operator are more common for string concatenation in modern JS.

toString() converts a value to its string representation. Example: (42).toString() returns '42'. Useful when you need to force a value to string type before calling string methods like .split() or checking .length.

length is a property (not a method) that gives the number of characters in a string. Example: 'hello'.length returns 5. Commonly used in validation: if(password.length < 8) showError(). Note: it counts UTF-16 code units, so some emoji may count as 2.

Any {} block — if statements, for loops, while loops — creates a new scope for let and const. Variables declared inside cannot be accessed outside. Example: for(let i=0; i<3; i++){} console.log(i); throws ReferenceError. This prevents loop variables from leaking into outer scope.

Variables declared outside any function or block are global and accessible everywhere. When looking up a variable, JS starts from the innermost scope and works outward until it finds it or throws ReferenceError. Example: const API_URL = 'https://...' at the top level is global, usable in all functions.

var is scoped to the nearest enclosing function, not to blocks. Example: if(true){ var x = 5; } console.log(x); works with var (x is accessible outside the if) but fails with let/const. This is why var can cause bugs in loops and conditionals.

Function declarations (function name(){}) are fully hoisted — the entire function name and body is available before its line in the code. Example: sayHello(); function sayHello(){ console.log('Hi'); } — this works because the declaration is hoisted to the top of the scope before execution starts.

Function expressions (const fn = function(){}) are not hoisted because the variable is let/const (temporal dead zone) or var (only var declaration hoisted, not its value). Example: sayHello(); const sayHello = () => {}; throws ReferenceError because sayHello is not yet initialized.

Arrow functions are always expressions assigned to variables, so they behave like function expressions — not hoisted. Example: greet(); const greet = () => 'Hi'; throws because greet is in the temporal dead zone when called. Always define arrow functions before using them.

map(callback) returns a new array where each element is the result of calling the callback on the original. Example: ['alice','bob'].map(name => name.toUpperCase()) returns ['ALICE','BOB']. The original array is unchanged. Perfect for transforming every item in a list.

forEach(callback) calls the callback for each element but returns undefined — it is for side effects, not for building new arrays. Example: [1,2,3].forEach(n => console.log(n)) logs each number. Use map instead if you need a transformed array back.

filter(callback) returns a new array containing only elements for which the callback returns true. Example: [1,2,3,4,5].filter(n => n%2===0) returns [2,4] — only the even numbers. The original array is unchanged.

reduce(callback, initialValue) processes each element and accumulates a single result. The callback receives the accumulator and current element. Example: [1,2,3,4].reduce((sum, n) => sum+n, 0) returns 10. Powerful for sums, counting, grouping, and flat-mapping.

every(fn) returns true only if ALL elements pass the test. some(fn) returns true if at least ONE element passes. Both short-circuit early. Example: [2,4,6].every(n => n%2===0) is true; [1,3,5].some(n => n>4) is true (5 > 4). Useful for validation checks on arrays.

reverse() reverses the array in place (mutates the original!). flat(depth) flattens nested arrays. Example: [1,[2,[3]]].flat(2) returns [1,2,3]. [1,2,3].reverse() mutates and returns [3,2,1]. Use [...arr].reverse() to get a reversed copy without mutation.
