Typical constraints: 2 ≤ n ≤ 10^5, -10^9 ≤ arr[i] ≤ 10^9. Must handle negative values: [-5,-1,-3] → second largest = -3. Initialize secondLargest = INT_MIN or -1 depending on the problem's value range.

If all elements are equal, return -1. If maximum appears multiple times, still find the second distinct value. Example: [9,9,9,3] → 3 (9 is the max; 3 is second). Key: track distinct values, not just positions.

sort(arr, arr+n); — after this, arr[n-1] is the maximum. Elements are now in order, making it easy to scan backwards for the next distinct value. Sorting handles duplicates naturally since all duplicates cluster together.

for(int i=n-2; i>=0; i--) if(arr[i]!=arr[n-1]) return arr[i];. The first element smaller than the max encountered going right-to-left is the second largest. Example: sorted [1,3,5,5,7] — arr[4]=7, scan: arr[3]=5 ≠ 7, return 5.
If the loop exits without finding a different element, all values are equal — return -1. Example: [4,4,4] — sorted is [4,4,4]; no element differs from arr[2]=4; return -1.

Sorting dominates: O(n log n) time. std::sort is in-place, so O(log n) stack space. If you mustn't modify the original array, make a copy first (O(n) space). Trade-off: simpler code vs worse time than the two-pass O(n) approach.
int mx=arr[0]; for(int i=1;i<n;i++) if(arr[i]>mx) mx=arr[i];. After this, mx holds the true maximum. Example: arr=[4,7,2,7,1]; mx=7. This first pass is just the standard linear max-finding algorithm.
int sec=-1; for(int i=0;i<n;i++) if(arr[i]!=mx && arr[i]>sec) sec=arr[i];. This finds the largest value that is strictly less than mx. Example: arr=[4,7,2,7,1], mx=7; candidates: 4,2,1; sec=4.

Starting sec at -1 means if no valid second largest exists (all elements equal to max), we return -1 naturally. If the problem allows negative values, initialize sec to INT_MIN instead and check separately if sec was updated.

Two separate loops each O(n) → O(n) total. Only two extra variables (mx, sec) → O(1) space. Strictly better time than the sorting approach with the same simplicity. This is typically the expected efficient solution.
int mx=INT_MIN, sec=INT_MIN; for each arr[i]: if(arr[i]>mx){sec=mx; mx=arr[i];} else if(arr[i]<mx && arr[i]>sec){sec=arr[i];}. Return sec if sec!=INT_MIN else -1. Single pass handles both updates.

Crucial order: when arr[i] > mx, first set sec = mx (the old max becomes second), then set mx = arr[i]. If you update mx first, you lose the old max value. This two-step update inside the loop is the key to correctness in one pass.

If arr[i] == mx, skip both updates (we want distinct second largest). If arr[i] == sec, also skip — it's already accounted for. Only update when strict inequalities hold. Example: [5,5,3] — both 5s hit the first condition but second 5 is not > mx(5), so sec stays as 3.

The single loop visits each element once — O(n). Uses only two extra variables — O(1) space. This is the most efficient approach. In interviews, presenting this after explaining brute-force and two-pass shows depth of understanding.

[5,5,5,5]: max=5, no element is strictly less than 5, so secondLargest is never updated from its initial -1. Return -1. This edge case is caught naturally by both two-pass and one-pass approaches if initialized correctly.
[9,3,9,7]: max=9 (appears twice). Second largest should be 7, not 9. Both two-pass (skips arr[i]==mx) and one-pass (else if arr[i]<mx) correctly ignore duplicate maxes and find 7.

Brute-force (sort): O(n log n) time, O(1) or O(n) space. Two-pass: O(n) time, O(1) space. One-pass: O(n) time, O(1) space. Two-pass and one-pass are equivalent asymptotically; one-pass has a lower constant factor. Sort is simplest to code but slowest.

n=2: [3,1] → 1; [3,3] → -1. Always initialize secondLargest to -1 (or INT_MIN for negative-value problems). For n=1 (if allowed), there's no second element — return -1 directly. Handle these base cases before the main loop.

if(arr[i]==1) cur++; — increment the current streak. The streak continues as long as consecutive 1s appear. Example: [1,1,1] → cur becomes 1,2,3. Tracking the current streak separately from the maximum lets us reset correctly on 0.
else cur=0; — when arr[i]==0, the streak breaks. Reset to 0 so the next group of 1s starts fresh. Example: [1,1,0,1] — after the 0, cur resets to 0, then increments to 1 for the final 1.

maxStreak = max(maxStreak, cur); — update after each increment. Placed inside the loop, this captures the streak length as it grows. Final maxStreak holds the answer. Example: [0,1,1,0,1,1,1] → maxStreak updates to 2, then 3.
arr=[1,1,0,1,1,1,0,1]: cur=0, maxS=0. i=0:cur=1,maxS=1; i=1:cur=2,maxS=2; i=2:cur=0; i=3:cur=1; i=4:cur=2; i=5:cur=3,maxS=3; i=6:cur=0; i=7:cur=1. Answer: 3. One pass O(n), two variables O(1).

for(int i=1;i<=n;i++){ bool found=false; for(int j=0;j<n-1;j++) if(arr[j]==i){found=true;break;} if(!found) return i; }. Outer loop n iterations, inner loop n-1 each → O(n^2). Correct but too slow for large n.

bool present[n+1]={false}; for(int x:arr) present[x]=true; for(int i=1;i<=n;i++) if(!present[i]) return i;. Mark each number seen; the unmarked index is missing. O(n) time, O(n) extra space for the boolean array.

After marking, scan present[1..n] and return the first false index. Exactly one index will be false since the array has n-1 distinct values from 1..n with exactly one missing. Example: arr=[3,1,2] (n=4 missing 4) → present[4]=false → return 4.
Brute-force: only for very small n (≤100) or in test cases. Checklist: when O(n) space is acceptable and simplicity matters. For O(1) space solutions, use the sum formula or XOR method instead.
If all numbers 1..n were present, their sum is n*(n+1)/2. Example: n=5 → expected = 15. This formula gives the sum in O(1) without any loop. Derived from Gauss's arithmetic series identity.
long long total=0; for(int x:arr) total+=x; return n*(n+1)/2 - total;. The missing number equals expected minus actual sum. Example: n=5, arr=[1,2,4,5] (missing 3): expected=15, actual=12, missing=3.
One loop summing the array is O(n). Only one extra variable (total) → O(1) space. Much better than checklist's O(n) space. This is typically the go-to approach for its simplicity and efficiency.

For large n (e.g., 10^5), n*(n+1)/2 ≈ 5×10^9 which overflows int. Always use long long: long long expected = (long long)n*(n+1)/2;. Similarly accumulate array sum as long long to avoid overflow mid-computation.

int xorAll=0; for(int x:arr) xorAll^=x; for(int i=1;i<=n;i++) xorAll^=i;. Each number 1..n appears once in arr (except the missing one) and once in the 1..n loop. XOR of any pair is 0, so only the missing number remains unpaired.
After both loops, xorAll holds the missing number. Example: n=4, arr=[1,3,4]: XOR arr: 1^3^4=6. XOR 1..4: 1^2^3^4=4. Combined: 6^4=2. Missing is 2. XOR is its own inverse, so paired elements vanish.

Two loops O(n) each → O(n) total. Only one XOR variable → O(1) space. Key advantage over sum method: no overflow risk since XOR operates at bit level with no magnitude growth. Works correctly for any n within type range.

The array has n-1 elements (one missing from 1..n). XOR loop from 1 to n covers all n numbers. Without explicitly going to n, the full range isn't covered and the result is wrong. Always verify loop bounds: for(int i=1; i<=n; i++), not i<n.

Given array, return true if non-decreasing (each element ≤ next). [1,2,2,3] → true (non-strictly sorted). [1,3,2] → false. [5] → true (single element is trivially sorted). [3,3,3] → true (all equal is non-decreasing).

for(int i=0;i<n-1;i++) if(arr[i]>arr[i+1]) return false; return true;. Compare adjacent pairs. If any adjacent pair violates non-decreasing order, immediately return false. If all pairs pass, return true.
Pseudocode: FOR i from 0 to n-2: IF arr[i]>arr[i+1]: RETURN false; RETURN true. C++: bool isSorted(int arr[],int n){for(int i=0;i<n-1;i++)if(arr[i]>arr[i+1])return false;return true;}.

One pass through n-1 adjacent pairs → O(n). No extra memory used → O(1). In the best case (first pair unsorted), we return immediately → Omega(1) best case. Overall O(n) worst case is optimal since every element must be checked at least once.

unordered_set<int> st(arr, arr+n); — constructs a set from the array, automatically keeping only unique elements. Or insert one by one: for(int x:arr) st.insert(x);. Set handles deduplication internally.

int i=0; for(int x:st) arr[i++]=x;. Copy set elements back into arr. Since the set is unordered, elements may not be in sorted order — this is why sorted array assumption matters; the optimal two-pointer approach relies on it.

Pseudocode: CREATE set from array; COPY set back to array; RETURN set.size(). C++: unordered_set<int>st(arr,arr+n); int i=0; for(int x:st)arr[i++]=x; return st.size();. Simple but uses O(n) extra space.

Inserting n elements into unordered_set: O(n) average (O(n^2) worst case with hash collisions). Copying back: O(k) ≤ O(n). Space: O(n) for the set. Compare with optimal two-pointer: O(n) time, O(1) space — better on space.

When arr[j] is a new unique value: increment i, then arr[i]=arr[j]. This builds the unique prefix in-place without extra memory. Example: [1,1,2,3,3]: i=0(1); j=2:arr[2]=2≠arr[0]=1 → i=1,arr[1]=2; j=3:3≠2 → i=2,arr[2]=3. Result: [1,2,3] with k=3.
Pseudocode: i=0; FOR j=1 to n-1: IF arr[j]!=arr[i]: i++; arr[i]=arr[j]; RETURN i+1. C++: int removeDups(int arr[],int n){int i=0;for(int j=1;j<n;j++)if(arr[j]!=arr[i])arr[++i]=arr[j];return i+1;}

Single pass with two pointers → O(n). Modifies array in-place → O(1) extra space. This is the optimal solution. Requires sorted input — if unsorted, sort first (O(n log n)) then apply two-pointer. Return value k = unique count.

Two-pointer duplicate removal only works on sorted arrays because duplicates must be adjacent. If arr=[1,3,1,2], the two-pointer fails. Always sort first or use a set-based approach for unsorted arrays. The problem typically guarantees sorted input.
All duplicates [5,5,5,5]: i stays at 0, no updates; returns i+1=1 — only one unique element. All unique [1,2,3,4]: i increments every step; returns n — all elements are unique, no changes made.

The function returns k=i+1 where i is the final position of the last unique element. The array's first k positions contain the unique values in sorted order. Callers use k to know how many elements are valid in the modified array.

Typical: 1 ≤ n ≤ 3*10^4, -10^4 ≤ arr[i] ≤ 10^4. Single element array: n=1 → always 1 unique element, return 1 without entering the loop. Negative values work correctly since the comparison arr[j]!=arr[i] handles any integer values.

Non-zero elements must appear in the same order as in the original array. Example: [3,0,1] → [3,1,0], not [1,3,0]. Sorting would give [0,1,3] — wrong. The stability constraint is what makes this problem interesting.
Input [0,0,1] → Output [1,0,0]. Input [4,2,0,5,0,1] → Output [4,2,5,1,0,0]. Non-zeros (4,2,5,1) keep their order; zeros fill the remaining positions at the end.
Typical: 1 ≤ n ≤ 10^5, -10^9 ≤ arr[i] ≤ 10^9. Zeros are arr[i]==0 regardless of sign. Non-negative-only constraints simplify code slightly but the algorithm works for any integers.
vector<int> nonZero; for(int x:arr) if(x!=0) nonZero.push_back(x);. This preserves relative order of non-zeros automatically since we iterate left to right. Example: [0,3,0,1] → nonZero=[3,1].
for(int i=0;i<nonZero.size();i++) arr[i]=nonZero[i]; for(int i=nonZero.size();i<n;i++) arr[i]=0;. First k positions get non-zeros; remaining n-k positions get zeros. Example: arr becomes [3,1,0,0].
void moveZeros(int arr[],int n){vector<int>nz;for(int x:arr)if(x!=0)nz.push_back(x);int i=0;for(int x:nz)arr[i++]=x;while(i<n)arr[i++]=0;}

Collecting non-zeros: O(n). Overwriting: O(n). Total O(n) time. The temporary vector holds up to n elements → O(n) extra space. For the optimal O(1) space solution, use the two-pointer in-place approach instead.

i starts at 0 (next slot for non-zero). j scans from 0 to n-1. When arr[j]!=0: swap(arr[i],arr[j]); i++;. i and j both advance, but i only moves when a non-zero is placed. j always advances regardless.

Swapping moves the non-zero element to position i and the zero (if any) to position j. This preserves relative order because j always moves left to right — non-zeros are encountered in original order and placed at consecutive i positions.

Since j scans left to right and we only place non-zeros at i in the order we find them, relative order is maintained. Example: [0,3,0,1]: j=0(zero, skip); j=1(3, swap arr[0] and arr[1], i=1); j=2(zero, skip); j=3(1, swap arr[1] and arr[3],i=2) → [3,1,0,0].
void moveZeros(int arr[],int n){int i=0;for(int j=0;j<n;j++)if(arr[j]!=0){swap(arr[i],arr[j]);i++;}}

Single scan with j from 0 to n-1 → O(n). Two extra pointers → O(1). This is optimal: you must examine each element at least once (Omega(n)), and we match that. No extra array needed, making it memory-efficient.

All zeros [0,0,0]: j scans but never triggers swap (arr[j]==0 always); i stays at 0; array unchanged → [0,0,0]. Correct. No zeros [1,2,3]: every j triggers swap with i; since i==j each time, swaps are self-swaps; array unchanged. Both cases handled correctly.

Stability is guaranteed because j moves strictly left to right and we place non-zeros in i in the order they appear. This is the same reason that stable sorting preserves equal-element order — direction and consistency ensure stability.

When no zeros have been seen yet, i==j for every step (both pointers advance together). Each swap is arr[j] with itself — a no-op. To skip: if(i!=j) swap(arr[i],arr[j]); This is a minor optimization that avoids redundant memory writes when the array has no leading zeros.

for(init; condition; update) body;. Init runs once. Condition checked before each iteration — if false, loop exits. Update runs after each iteration. Example: for(int i=1;i<=5;i++) cout<<i; prints 1 2 3 4 5. All three parts are optional (infinite loop: for(;;)).

while(condition){ body; } — checks condition first; body may never run if false initially. do{ body; }while(condition); — runs body once before checking. Use do-while when body must execute at least once, like a menu prompt.

All three components of a for loop work together: init sets the starting state; condition guards entry; update moves toward termination. Forgetting the update or using the wrong condition direction creates infinite loops. Example: for(int i=10;i>0;i--) counts down.

break; immediately exits the innermost loop or switch. Example: for(int i=0;i<n;i++){if(arr[i]==target){cout<<i;break;}} — stops searching after finding the target. Without break, the loop continues needlessly. In nested loops, break exits only the innermost.
continue; skips the remaining body of the current iteration and jumps to the next. Example: for(int i=0;i<10;i++){if(i%2==0) continue; cout<<i;} — prints only odd numbers by skipping even ones.

In a while loop: while(i<n){if(arr[i]==0){i++;continue;} process(arr[i]); i++;}. If you forget i++ before continue, i never advances when arr[i]==0, creating an infinite loop. Always ensure the loop variable updates before continue.

int arr[5]; allocates five contiguous integers. int arr[]={1,2,3}; infers size as 3. All elements are adjacent in memory, enabling O(1) indexed access. Vector<int> v(n) is the dynamic, safer alternative in modern C++.

arr[i] reads element i (0-indexed). arr[i]=val; writes to it. Both are O(1). Accessing arr[n] or beyond is undefined behavior — no runtime error in C++, but likely crashes or corrupts data. Always guard indices within [0, n-1].

int mat[3][4]; declares a 3-row, 4-column matrix. Access: mat[r][c]. Stored in row-major order: mat[0][0],mat[0][1],...,mat[0][3],mat[1][0],... Example: mat[1][2] accesses row 1, column 2 — the 7th element in memory (1*4+2=6, zero-indexed).

int add(int a, int b){ return a+b; } — return type int, name add, params a and b. void greet(){ cout<<"Hi"; } — no return value. Call as: int sum=add(3,4); or greet();. The function call passes arguments and receives the return value.

Declaration (prototype): int add(int,int); — tells compiler the function exists, placed before main(). Definition: int add(int a,int b){return a+b;} — the actual implementation, often after main(). Separating them allows calling functions before defining them.

Built-in: provided by standard library (sort, max, cin, cout). User-defined: written by the programmer for specific tasks. Example: using sort() is built-in; writing your own bubbleSort() is user-defined. Both follow the same call syntax.

void square(int x){ x*=x; } — changes to x don't affect the caller. A copy of the argument is made. Example: int a=5; square(a); cout<<a; still prints 5. Use pass by value for primitives when you don't want to modify the caller's variable.

void increment(int& x){ x++; } — modifies the caller's variable. The & makes x an alias. Example: int a=5; increment(a); cout<<a; prints 6. Use references to avoid expensive copies of large objects and when mutation of the original is needed.

void print(int arr[], int n) — arr decays to a pointer to the first element. Modifications to arr inside the function affect the original array. Size information is lost — always pass n separately. Example: print(myArr, 5); passes the array by reference implicitly.

Containers: store data (vector, list, map, set). Algorithms: operate on ranges (sort, find, count). Iterators: bridge containers and algorithms (like pointers to elements). Example: sort(v.begin(), v.end()) uses iterators from a container to call an algorithm.

Functors are objects that act like functions (overload operator()). They allow algorithms to be customized. Example: sort(v.begin(),v.end(),greater<int>()) uses greater<int> as a functor to sort in descending order. Generic programming means code works for any type.

Use STL: when standard operations (CRUD, sorting, searching) on common structures are needed. Custom: when you need special behavior, tighter memory control, or the STL doesn't have what you need (e.g., a disjoint set union / DSU). STL covers 90% of interview needs.

pair<int,double> p = {3, 3.14}; p.first is 3, p.second is 3.14. Create with make_pair(3,3.14) or brace initialization {3,3.14}. Pairs support comparison: first by .first, then by .second — useful for sorted containers and priority queues.
pair<int,pair<int,int>> triple = {1,{2,3}}; — access third value as triple.second.second. vector<pair<int,int>> vp; stores many pairs. Common pattern in weighted graph edges: {weight, {u,v}}.

map<int,string> stores entries as pair<const int,string>. Use pairs as map keys: map<pair<int,int>,string> for 2D grid point mapping. Return two values from a function: pair<int,int> minMax(vector<int>& v) returns both min and max.

vector<int> v; — empty vector. vector<int> v(5,0); — five zeros. Internally maintains a pointer, size (current count), and capacity (allocated space). When size exceeds capacity, vector doubles its capacity and moves all elements (amortized O(1) push_back).

push_back(val) copies or moves val into the vector. emplace_back(args...) constructs the element in-place using args — avoids an extra copy. For complex objects, emplace_back is more efficient. For primitives, they are equivalent.

v[i] — direct access, no bounds check, O(1). v.at(i) — bounds-checked, throws out_of_range if i≥size(), O(1). v.front() — first element. v.back() — last element. Use at() during debugging to catch index errors; use [] in production for speed.

v.begin() → iterator to first element. v.end() → iterator past last. v.rbegin() → reverse iterator to last element. v.rend() → reverse iterator before first. Use rbegin/rend to iterate backwards. Example: for(auto it=v.rbegin();it!=v.rend();it++) prints in reverse.

Iterator types can be verbose: vector<int>::iterator it. Use auto to let the compiler deduce: auto it=v.begin();. auto simplifies code significantly. Const iterators (cbegin/cend) prevent modification of elements — good for read-only traversal.

Range-based (simpler): for(int x:v) cout<<x; — clean, reads each element. Iterator-based: for(auto it=v.begin();it!=v.end();it++) cout<<*it; — gives access to the iterator itself (useful for erasing, inserting, or position-aware logic).

v.insert(v.begin()+i, val) — insert at position i, O(n). v.insert(v.begin()+i, count, val) — insert count copies. v.insert(v.end(), other.begin(), other.end()) — append a range. All shift existing elements right — O(n) cost for non-end insertions.

v.erase(v.begin()+i) — removes element at index i, O(n). v.erase(v.begin()+i, v.begin()+j) — removes elements [i,j), O(n). Elements after the erased position shift left. Use erase with caution inside loops — iterator invalidation can cause bugs.

v.size() — element count O(1). v.pop_back() — removes last element O(1). v.swap(v2) — swaps contents O(1). v.clear() — removes all elements, size becomes 0 O(n). v.empty() — true if no elements O(1). These utility methods cover most vector management needs.
Match the correct form of 'be' with the subject, then add 'able to' and the base verb. Example: 'I am able to speak three languages.' Always use the base verb (not -ing or past form).
I am able to lead the project. She is able to write reports clearly. We are able to deliver the product on schedule. These show how the subject changes but the structure stays the same.
Put 'not' directly after the form of 'be.' Example: 'They are not able to process the request at this time.' Simple and clear for formal writing.
I am not able to download the file. She is not able to join the call. We are not able to guarantee delivery by Friday. Each sentence shows a specific limitation at the present moment.

'Can' is informal and only works in present tense. 'Able to' works in all tenses and suits formal writing. Example: Résumé — 'I am able to lead cross-functional teams' sounds more professional than 'I can lead teams.'
Always match 'be' to the subject. 'He is able to' not 'He are able to.' In past tense: 'She was able to' not 'She were able to.' A common exam error is mismatching the subject and helping verb.
When something is happening right now or was happening at a specific past moment, use continuous tense. Example: 'The students are writing their exams' shows an action in progress at this moment.
All continuous tenses require verb + -ing. Drop silent 'e' before adding -ing (write → writing). Example: 'She is running the simulation.' The -ing form paired with 'be' is the formula to remember.
Choose the right form of 'be' for the subject, then add verb-ing. Example: 'I am preparing the slides.' 'They are testing the software.' A simple and clean formula.
I am reading the manual. She is debugging the code. We are discussing the timeline. These three show how the subject changes while the -ing structure stays constant.
Place 'not' after 'am/is/are' and before the verb-ing. Example: 'The system is not responding at the moment.' This structure clearly shows the action is not in progress.
He is not watching the tutorial. They are not attending the event. I am not eating at the cafeteria today. Each example shows a temporary non-action happening right now.
Past continuous is often used to show an action that was interrupted. Example: 'She was presenting her report when the power went out.' The main action (presenting) was interrupted by a sudden event.
Future continuous shows an action that will be in progress at a specific future time. Example: 'At 10 am tomorrow, the board will be reviewing our proposal.'
Continuous tenses work well for temporary and changing situations. Example: 'Traffic is increasing every year' shows an ongoing trend. 'I am staying with my cousin this month' is temporary.
Always follow a modal with a base verb — never add -ing, -s, or -ed. Example: 'She should attend' (correct) vs 'She should attending' (wrong). This rule applies to all modals without exception.

These nine modals are the most tested in exams. Each has a primary use: can (ability), may (permission), must (obligation), should (advice), will (future/certainty). Memorising the primary use of each saves time in exams.
Modals serve six main functions. Example — ability: 'I can swim'; permission: 'May I leave?'; necessity: 'You must wear a helmet.' Matching function to modal is the main exam challenge.
Use may for something that is likely possible; use might for something less certain. Example: 'She may win the award' (fairly possible) vs 'She might come if she finishes early' (uncertain).
'May' is the polite and formal choice for permission. Example: 'May I use the conference room?' is more formal than 'Can I use it?' Teachers and managers often prefer 'may' in professional settings.
'May not' expresses either denial of permission or uncertainty. Example: 'You may not enter without a pass' (denial). 'She may not attend' (uncertain).
Questions with 'may' are formal and polite. Example: 'May we begin the presentation?' or 'May I ask a question?' These are common in professional and academic settings.
'Shall' used with I or we shows strong intention or a promise. Example: 'We shall not compromise on quality.' It sounds more formal and emphatic than 'will.'
Questions with 'Shall I/we?' are soft offers. Example: 'Shall I schedule a meeting for Monday?' This is common in professional emails and conversations.
Positive: 'I shall prepare the report.' Negative: 'We shall not delay the submission.' The negative adds emphasis — it is a strong statement of non-action.
'Should' gives advice without forcing an action. Example: 'You should review your notes before the exam.' It implies it is a good idea but the choice is yours.
'Should' can also soften requests. Example: 'You should contact the helpdesk if the issue persists.' It is gentler than 'must' and widely used in professional communication.
Positive: 'She should complete the training.' Negative: 'Students should not skip the mandatory session.' The negative 'should not' is a strong recommendation to avoid something.
'Must' signals that something is required or absolutely necessary. Example: 'You must submit the form before the deadline.' There is no choice involved — it is compulsory.
'Must not' is a prohibition — a firm rule against something. Example: 'You must not share your password with anyone.' It is stronger than 'should not.'
Students must carry their ID cards. All participants must register online. The driver must wear a seatbelt. These examples show 'must' in contexts of rules and requirements.
When you describe experiences without saying exactly when they happened, use Present Perfect. Example: 'I have tried sushi.' The exact time does not matter — the experience is what counts.
A past action that still affects the present. Example: 'He has sprained his ankle' — the injury happened in the past but he is still affected now. The present result is key.
Signal words like just, already, and yet indicate very recent events. Example: 'She has just submitted the report.' The action happened moments ago and is still fresh.
Use Present Perfect to show how things have evolved. Example: 'Smartphones have transformed communication.' It marks a change that started in the past and is still evident now.
The basic formula. V3 is the past participle — third form of the verb. Example: go → gone, write → written, finish → finished. 'They have finished the installation.'
This passive form focuses on what was done rather than who did it. Example: 'The invoice has been sent' focuses on the result, not on who sent it. Common in professional writing.
Negative: 'He has not submitted the form.' Question: 'Have they completed the test?' Just add 'not' for negatives and invert have/has and subject for questions.
Just = very recently. Already = sooner than expected. Yet = up to now (questions/negatives). Example: 'She has just arrived.' 'He has already eaten.' 'Have they left yet?'
Ever = at any time (questions/negatives). Never = not at any time. Example: 'Have you ever ridden a horse?' 'I have never missed a deadline.' Both describe experiences over a lifetime.
For = a period of time. Since = a point in time. Example: 'He has worked here for three years.' 'She has been here since Monday.' A very common exam trap — mixing up for and since.
These express ongoing timeframes up to now. Example: 'So far, we have raised 50% of the target.' 'I have not been feeling well lately.' Great for progress reports and updates.
'Always' with Present Perfect shows a long-standing habit. 'Until now' marks a change point. Example: 'She has always been punctual.' 'Until now, no one has questioned the policy.'
The defining feature of this tense is continuity — the action is still happening. Example: 'They have been renovating the building for two months.' The renovation is not finished yet.
Use this tense when duration matters. Example: 'I have been working on this project since Monday.' The emphasis is on the stretch of time, not just the fact of working.
For he/she/it, use 'has been' + verb-ing. Example: 'He has been practising the presentation since morning.' The singular form 'has' pairs with one person or thing.
For I, you, we, and they, use 'have been' + verb-ing. Example: 'We have been discussing the budget since 9 am.' The plural form 'have' applies to groups or to 'I'.
She has been running the lab for three years. They have been collecting data since January. These show the action stretched over a clear timeframe with no end yet.
For he/she/it, the negative is 'has not been' + verb-ing. Example: 'She has not been responding to emails since last week.' This shows an ongoing absence of action.
For I/you/we/they, use 'have not been' + verb-ing. Example: 'They have not been meeting the targets since the restructure.' This points to a sustained period of non-performance.
I have not been sleeping well lately. The team has not been communicating effectively. These sentences show ongoing issues, not single missed events.
For = how long (a period): 'for two weeks,' 'for six months.' Since = when it started (a point): 'since Monday,' 'since 2020.' Both show the action is still going on.
He has been working on the proposal for three days. I have been learning Spanish since January. These show realistic use of Present Perfect Continuous in professional and personal contexts.
Use this when an action is done and the result matters now. Example: 'The team has delivered the prototype.' The delivery happened and the prototype now exists — a clear present result.
Use this to describe a state or role that has been ongoing. Example: 'She has been the head of research for two years.' The state (head of research) started two years ago and still continues.
The past action still matters right now. Example: 'He has just signed the contract' — the signing is done, but the contract is now active. Present Perfect highlights that relevance.
When a situation started in the past and is still true, use Present Perfect. Example: 'She has been the team leader since 2021.' The role is ongoing — it has not ended.

Present Perfect with state verbs or 'has been' captures long-standing identity. Example: 'This university has been a centre of research excellence for decades.' The characteristic has accumulated over time.
He/she/it → has been + verb-ing. I/you/we/they → have been + verb-ing. Example: 'The department has been recruiting new staff.' 'We have been reviewing the feedback.'
This tense is ideal when the action is still ongoing. Example: 'I have been preparing for the presentation since Monday.' It started on Monday and the preparation is still in progress.
Choose Present Perfect when the action is finished and its result or the experience itself is the point. Example: 'He has earned three certifications.' The certifications exist — the action is done.
Choose Perfect Continuous when the duration or ongoing nature is the focus. Example: 'She has been studying for the board exam for six months.' Six months of continuous effort — not yet done.
Use Simple Present for habits, routines, and general truths. Structure: V1 (base verb) or V1+s/es for he/she/it. Example: 'Water boils at 100 degrees Celsius.' 'She works at the research lab.'

Use Simple Past for completed actions at a specific time. Structure: V2 (past form). Example: 'The team launched the product last month.' Time markers like yesterday, last week, and in 2020 often appear.

Use Simple Future for predictions, decisions, or plans. Structure: will + V1 or am/is/are + going to + V1. Example: 'I will submit the report tomorrow.' 'They are going to expand the team next quarter.'

Simple Present negative: 'She does not attend the weekly meeting.' Simple Past negative: 'He did not complete the assignment.' Simple Future negative: 'They will not delay the launch.' Each uses the right auxiliary.
Shows what is happening right now or around now. Structure: am/is/are + verb-ing. Example: 'The developers are testing the new feature.' Also used for temporary arrangements.
Shows what was in progress at a specific past moment, often interrupted by another event. Structure: was/were + verb-ing. Example: 'They were reviewing the data when the server crashed.'
Shows what will be in progress at a specific future time. Structure: will be + verb-ing. Example: 'At noon tomorrow, the CEO will be addressing the press conference.'
Present negative: 'She is not attending the session.' Past negative: 'We were not monitoring the system when it failed.' Future negative: 'He will not be joining the call at that time.'
Simple Present: subject + base verb. Add -s/-es for he/she/it. Example: 'I work late.' 'She works late.' Getting the -s/es right is a common exam error to watch for.
Simple tense negatives and questions use do/does (present) or did (past). Example: 'Does she submit reports on time?' 'He did not complete the test.' Never add 'do' with continuous tenses.
For passive or result focus: am/is/are/was/were + V3. Example: 'The application was processed yesterday.' Negative: 'The request was not approved.' Widely used in formal reports.
Will + base verb = spontaneous decision or prediction. Be going to + base verb = planned or intended action. Example: 'I will call you back' (just decided) vs 'I am going to call you at 5' (planned).
